coredata.dc:title: Construction of sub-10nm ultra-thin polyamide layer using porous GOQDs-AGQDs interlayer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Yumin
Liu, Hailiang
Cui, Hao
Li, Yang
Sun, Chengxu
Chen, Kaikai
Huang, Qinglin
Xiao, Changfa
coredata.dc:description: The application of high-performance polyamide nanofiltration membranes with controllable structures shows promising prospects in fields such as seawater desalination and wastewater treatment. In this study, a high-performance polyamide composite nanofiltration membrane with sub-10nm ultra-thin separation layer was designed and fabricated using a hydrophilic porous GOQDs-AGQDs (graphene oxide quantum dots-amino graphene quantum dots) interlayer to reconstruct the substrate architecture. The GOQDs-AGQDs interlayer not only improved the uniformity and smoothness of the microporous substrate, but also could form hydrogen bond with amine monomers to effectively inhibit its diffusion, thereby effectively slowing down the interfacial polymerization process and facilitating the formation of an ultra-thin, smooth and dense polyamide (PA) layer. The results demonstrate that the optimized PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES membrane achieved a PA separation layer thickness as low as 9.4 nm, with a permeation flux of 27.2 L·m−2 ·h−1·bar−1, nearly three times that of the original PA/PES membrane. Simultaneously, it enhanced the Na2SO4 rejection rate to 97.1 %, achieving synchronous improvements in permeability and selectivity. Additionally, the PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane exhibited relatively low surface roughness and demonstrated excellent long-term stability. This work presents a novel strategy for the controllable fabrication of high-performance nanofiltration membranes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GOQDs-AGQDs interlayer
Nanofiltration membrane
Sub-10nm ultra-thin PA layer
Structural regulation

We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University for the test analysis work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation of PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane.
FESEM and AFM images of membranes surfaces.
FESEM images of the membrane cross-section with the thicknesses of the interlayers and PA separation layers.
Chemical structure and surface properties of membranes: (a) FTIR spectra; (b) XPS spectra; (c–f) High resolution C1s spectra of four composite membranes. (g) Zeta potential; (h) Water contact angles.

(a) and (b) Separation performance of PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane for Na2SO4 under different IP conditions. Separation performance of NF membranes with different interlayers (4 bar): (c) Na2SO4 rejection and corresponding permeation fluxes; (d–e) rejection and permeance of different salts; (f) Comparison of desalination performance of PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES membranes with various NF membranes with interlayer in the literature (details were given in Table S2).
Schematic of water transport nanochannels in GOQDs-AGQDs, GOQDs and GO interlayers.
Filtration stability of membranes: (a) PA/PES; (b) PA/GO/PES; (c) PA/GOQDs/PES; (d) PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES.

head.ce:title: Construction of sub-10nm ultra-thin polyamide layer using porous GOQDs-AGQDs interlayer

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The application of high-performance polyamide nanofiltration membranes with controllable structures shows promising prospects in fields such as seawater desalination and wastewater treatment. In this study, a high-performance polyamide composite nanofiltration membrane with sub-10nm ultra-thin separation layer was designed and fabricated using a hydrophilic porous GOQDs-AGQDs (graphene oxide quantum dots-amino graphene quantum dots) interlayer to reconstruct the substrate architecture. The GOQDs-AGQDs interlayer not only improved the uniformity and smoothness of the microporous substrate, but also could form hydrogen bond with amine monomers to effectively inhibit its diffusion, thereby effectively slowing down the interfacial polymerization process and facilitating the formation of an ultra-thin, smooth and dense polyamide (PA) layer. The results demonstrate that the optimized PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES membrane achieved a PA separation layer thickness as low as 9.4 nm, with a permeation flux of 27.2 L·m−2 ·h−1·bar−1, nearly three times that of the original PA/PES membrane. Simultaneously, it enhanced the Na2SO4 rejection rate to 97.1 %, achieving synchronous improvements in permeability and selectivity. Additionally, the PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane exhibited relatively low surface roughness and demonstrated excellent long-term stability. This work presents a novel strategy for the controllable fabrication of high-performance nanofiltration membranes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Membrane separation technology holds immense promise in seawater desalination and wastewater treatment due to its high separation efficiency and low energy consumption. Within the family of membranes, nanofiltration (NF) membranes play an essential role due to their distinctive separation capabilities (rejecting divalent ions and specific organic molecules) and their practical advantages, including cost-effectiveness, ease of operation, and scalability [1,2]. At present, thin film composite (TFC) nanofiltration (NF) membranes, fabricated by the interfacial polymerization (IP) of piperazine (PIP) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) on a porous substrate, have attained considerable success and now dominate the NF membrane market. However, the IP process of PIP and TMC monomers is relatively rapid and uncontrollable, resulting in non-uniform pore sizes and thicker PA separation layers in TFC-NF membranes, thereby preventing simultaneous improvements in permeability and ion selectivity [3,4].

The separation performance of TFC membranes is intimately connected to the structural properties of the PA layer. Over the past few years, various methods have been implemented to optimize IP reactions and fabricate PA separation layers with desirable properties. These approaches encompass the alteration of monomers [5,6], surface modifications [7,8], the introduction of additives into either the aqueous or organic phases [9–11], the optimization of preparation processes [12], and the incorporation of interlayers [13,14]. Among these, introducing an interlayer between the porous substrate and the PA nanolayer has proven to be an effective approach for enhancing membrane permeability [15].

Advancements in interlayered thin film composite (i-TFC) membranes have shown that nanomaterials such as graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [16,17], cellulose nanocrystals (CNC) [18,19], metal-organic frameworks [20,21], and covalent organic frameworks [22,23] can serve as effective interlayers. These interlayers modify the surface properties and pore structure of the substrate membrane, influencing the storage and release of PIP monomers within the substrate membrane and regulating the formation of the PA layer. Gui et al. [24] demonstrated a pre-formed g-C3N4 nanofiber network membrane using a self-deposition process before the formation of the PA layer. Results showed that this unique g-C3N4 nanofiber network membrane exhibited extremely high hydrophilicity, high porosity and a relatively smooth surface, facilitating the formation of defect-free and wrinkle PA separation layer. Despite enhancing the storage of amine monomers on the substrate to some extent, these hydrophilic nanomaterials are inherently limited in their ability to control amine monomer diffusion due to large voids, thereby resulting in a relatively rapid IP process [25].

Recently, graphene oxide (GO) sheets have garnered significant attention in the realm of NF membranes owing to their outstanding hydrophilicity and the “tortuous effect” on the diffusion of loaded molecules [26–28]. Zhang et al. [29] utilized polyvinyl alcohol-modified graphene oxide as an interlayer, followed by further glutaraldehyde crosslinking, to control IP reactions, resulting in a high-flux, ultra-thin and super-smooth thin film nanocomposite membrane. Lai et al. [30] employed GO as an interlayer to modulate the surface hydrophilicity of the PA layer by adjusting the loading amount, so as to improve the attraction of the membrane surface to water molecules, thereby enhancing the anti-fouling performance while increasing permeation flux. However, GO membrane with long and tortuous transport pathways are prone to be compacted in the filtration process, which are detrimental to enhancing permeability-selectivity [31]. Opting for smaller-sized graphene-based two-dimensional nanosheets could offer more vertical nanochannels and relatively lower tortuous transport paths, thereby optimizing the storage and release of amine monomers to regulate IP reactions. Additionally, in contrast to large GO sheet interlayer, different smaller nanosheets self-assembled into parallel stacks create numerous parallel and vertical nanochannels, forming multidimensional nanochannels that increase free volume and enhance mass transfer [32–34].

In our previous study [35], graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs, size<100 nm) and amino graphene quantum dots (AGQDs, size<10 nm), as nanoscale fragments of two-dimensional GO sheets, were assembled into a lamellar membrane (GOQDs-AGQDs composite membrane). This membrane possesses abundant horizontal and vertical nanochannels, which can form numerous in-plane gaps and interlayer pathways, partially alleviating the difficulty of interlayer storage of amine monomers. Furthermore, the plentiful hydrophilic groups on the AGQDs and GOQDs nanosheets can facilitate the hydrogen bonding interactions with the amine monomers, which in turn regulate the diffusion rate of amine monomers and enable precise control the IP.

In this study, a novel interlayered TFC membrane with a smooth and ultra-thin PA layer structure was designed by introducing GOQDs-AGQDs composite interlayer with abundant hydrophilic groups to regulate the IP process. The presence of the GOQDs-AGQDs interlayer can create uniform nanopore and lamellar structures on the substrate membrane surface and enhance interactions with amine monomers by improving the surface hydrophilicity of the substrate membrane, thereby slowing down amine monomer diffusion and making the IP process more controllable and uniform. By comparing different interlayers and IP conditions, the morphology, chemical structure, surface charge, hydrophilicity and desalination performance of the PA/GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes were investigated. This GOQDs-AGQDs interlayer with multidimensional nanochannels provides a new approach for designing high-performance TFC-NF membranes.

Polyethersulfone (PES) ultrafiltration membranes (pore size: 0.1 μm) were provided by Jiangsu Haiyan New Oriental Technology Co., Ltd. Graphene oxide (GO, single layer ratio >98 %) originated from Soochow Hengqiu Graphene Technology Co. Ltd., USA. H2O2 and Na2SO4 were obtained from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. MgSO4, MgCl2, NaCl, n-hexane and NH3·H2O were purchased from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. TMC and PIP were sourced from Beijing J&K Scientific Ltd.

Building upon our previous work [35], GOQDs and AGQDs were synthesized from GO using a “top-down” approach with H2O2 and NH3·H2O, respectively (Figs. S1–S3). Then a mixed aqueous solution of GOQDs and AGQDs with a total volume of 1.0 mL was prepared (0.01w/v%), where the mass fraction of AGQDs was 60 wt%. The mixed solution was diluted to 50 mL with distilled water, then filtered under −0.03 MPa negative pressure onto a PES substrate membrane. After heat treatment at 45 °C for 10 min, the GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane was fabricated.

Aqueous solutions of PIP (0.02, 0.04, 0.06 w/v%) and a TMC/n-hexane solution (0.1 w/v%) were prepared for IP. The GOQDs-AGQDs/PES membrane was placed in a cultivate dish and immersed in a PIP aqueous solution for 10 min. Subsequently, the GOQDs-AGQDs/PES membrane was extracted from the solution and positioned on a glass plate, where any surplus PIP solution was eliminated with a rubber roller. It was then subjected to IP with TMC/n-hexane solution for specified time (20, 40, 60s). Finally, the membranes were heat-treated at 45 °C for 10 min in an oven to produce a series of PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membranes under different IP conditions. The preparation process was illustrated in Fig. 1 .

By comparison, the PES substrate membrane served as a blank sample in the IP process, while the GO/PES membrane and GOQDs/PES membrane were prepared with the same loading amount (143.0 mg·m−2) as the GOQDs-AGQDs/PES membrane and subjected to the IP process. These membranes were respectively named PA/PES, PA/GO/PES, PA/GOQDs/PES and PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS50, ThermoFisher, USA) was employed to analyze the surface functional groups of the composite membranes. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-alpha, Thermo Fisher, USA) was used to analyze the elemental composition of the composite membranes. Cold field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, S4800, Hitachi, Japan) was utilized to characterize the surface and cross-sectional morphologies of the membranes. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Icon, USA) was utilized to characterize the surface roughness of the membranes. Surface zeta potential and water contact angle were determined using a solid surface zeta potential analyzer (SURPASS-3, Anton Paar, Austria) and a contact angle meter (Kruss DSA-100, Germany).

Under 4 bar operating pressure and room temperature, the permeability of the prepared composite membranes was evaluated using a lab-made cross-flow filtration system. 2 g ·L−1 Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2 and NaCl solutions were separately prepared for desalination performance testing. The membranes (effective area = 7.0 cm2) were pre-pressed under 4 bar for 30 min. Subsequently, membrane permeation flux (J) and rejection (R) were calculated according to Eqs. (1) and (2):
(1)
J =
V
T · A · Δ P
Where J, V, T, A, and ΔP represented permeation flux (L·m−2 ·h−1 ·bar−1), volume of permeate (L), test duration (h), effective area of the membrane (m2), and operating pressure (bar), respectively.
(2)
R =
C f
−
C p
C f
· 100 %
Where C
f and C
p were the feed and permeate concentration, respectively. The concentration of the salt solutions was indirectly determined using a conductivity meter (OHAUS ST3100C).

To assess the stability of the membranes, long-term NF performance tests were conducted on the prepared composite membranes using a 2 g·L-1 Na2SO4 aqueous solution. The membranes were operated continuously for 20 h under a pressure of 4 bar, with measurements of permeation flux and Na2SO4 rejection taken every 2 h. These measurements were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2).

FESEM and AFM images of composite membranes surfaces with various interlayers were presented in Fig. 2 . As depicted in the images, the PES substrate membrane exhibited an uneven large pore structure, whereas the GO/PES, GOQDs/PES and GOQDs-AGQDs/PES membranes with various interlayers displayed uniform surfaces without evident open pore structures. After the PA layer covered, the PA/PES composite membrane showed a surface with numerous irregular accumulations and protrusions, which formed a vesicle-like structure [36,37]. This phenomenon arose because the IP reaction between PIP and TMC was exothermic, and the traditional operation of immersing in the aqueous phase first followed by the organic phase was unfavorable for heat dissipation. The inability to timely transfer the heat led to the formation of vesicle structures. For the PA/GO/PES, PA/GOQDs/PES and PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membranes with various interlayers, the surfaces were all relatively smooth. This phenomenon was attributed to the introduction of the interlayer, which reduced the pore size of the substrate membrane surface and enhanced the surface hydrophilicity. These hydrophilic groups (such as –COOH, –OH and –NH2) could form hydrogen bonds with PIP molecules in the aqueous phase (Figs. S4–5). And the combined reduced pore structure significantly reduced the IP reaction rate, inhibiting the occurrence of vesicle structures. Additionally, the AFM images revealed that the PA/GO/PES, PA/GOQDs/PES and PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES membranes (with surface roughness of Ra = 27.2 nm, Ra = 25.1 nm and Ra = 23.7 nm) with various interlayers had lower roughness compared with the PA/PES membrane (Ra = 47.6 nm), which was consistent with the FESEM images.

Fig. 3 presented cross-sectional FESEM images of the composite membranes. These images clearly illustrated that the functional layer thickness of these four membranes varied depending on the types of interlayers, and the resulting composite membranes exhibited relatively thin functional layers. Before IP, the functional layer thicknesses of the GO/PES, GOQDs/PES and GOQDs-AGQDs/PES membranes were 27.7 nm, 27.2 nm and 23.6 nm, respectively. After IP, the PA/GO/PES, PA/GOQDs/PES and PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membranes with interlayer exhibited lower functional layer thicknesses (37.5 nm, 36.2 nm and 32.9 nm, respectively, including interlayer and PA separation layer) than the PA/PES composite membrane (66.4 nm) without interlayer. Calculations revealed that the thicknesses of the PA separation layers in these three composite membranes were 9.8 nm, 9.0 nm, and 9.3 nm, all below 10 nm. This reduction was attributed to the role of the GO, GOQDs and GOQDs-AGQDs interlayers in replacing the PES substrate membrane to store and release PIP molecules.

Compared to the uneven large pore structure on the surface of the PES substrate membrane, the uniform small pore structure and hydrophilic properties of the interlayer facilitated better control over the diffusion of PIP molecules, thereby reducing the IP rate and forming thinner PA layers within the same time [38,39]. The smooth membrane surface and thinner PA layer undoubtedly contributed to improved membrane fouling resistance and permeability.

To investigate the influence of different interlayers on the chemical structure of the PA layer, FTIR and XPS were employed to characterize the GOQDs/PES, GOQDs-AGQDs/PES, PA/PES, PA/GO/PES, PA/GOQDs/PES and PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membranes (Fig. S7, Fig. S8, Fig. 4(a-f)). As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the absorption peak appearing at 3500-3200 cm−1 was attributed to the superposition of –NH and –OH groups, including the stretching vibrations of –NH in the amide bond and the stretching vibrations of –OH in –COOH generated by hydrolysis of TMC [40]. In addition, characteristic peaks corresponding to CO stretching vibrations of amide bonds at 1630 cm−1 and C–N stretching vibrations at 1410 cm−1 were observed, these peaks indicated the successful reaction of PIP and TMC on the interlayer surface to form the PA layer [41–43]. Due to the micrometer-scale detection depth of FTIR, the intensity of these characteristic peaks was relatively weak, which indirectly confirming the formation of a thin PA layer.

From Fig. 4(b), it can be observed that the four prepared composite membranes only contained the elements C, O and N, without sulfur (S) element from the PES substrate membrane. This was attributed to the nanometer-scale detection depth of XPS, which indirectly confirmed the formation of the complete and defect-free composite functional layers. The relative elemental compositions of the four composite membranes listed in Table S1 showed that the PA/GOQDs/PES and PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membranes had higher oxygen content, which might be related to the presence of GOQDs and GOQDs-AGQDs interlayers. In addition, compared to the GOQDs/PES and GOQDs-AGQDs/PES substrate membrane (Figs. S8b–d), PA/GOQDs/PES and PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membranes had higher N content, which was related to the formation of PA layer. Compared to the PA/PES composite membrane, the GOQDs and GOQDs-AGQDs interlayers had uniform small pore structures and hydrophilic groups, which could control the uniform diffusion of PIP molecules to the reaction interface. Moreover, the abundant hydrophilic groups in the interlayer formed hydrogen bonds with PIP, further slowing down the PIP diffusion rate and enhancing the cross-linking degree. However, as shown in Fig. 3, the formed PA layers were relatively thin, which was lower than the detection depth of XPS (about 10 nm), and the interlayer containing oxygen-rich functional groups could influence the XPS detection results, resulting in a much higher oxygen content than that of the PA/PES composite membrane. Desalination performance tests also indicated that the presence of an interlayer facilitated effective control over the IP process, leading to the formation of ultra-thin and dense PA layers, thereby improving the selectivity performance. As depicted in Fig. 4(c–f), peak fitting of the C1s spectra revealed characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV (CC/C–C), 285.8 eV (C–N) and 288.0 eV (N–CO/O–CO), which indicated the presence of typical polyamide structures in the prepared composite membranes [44]. In addition, the O1s and N1s of the four composite membranes were shown in Fig. S7.

Fig. 4(g) presented the surface zeta potentials of the PA/PES, PA/GO/PES, PA/GOQDs/PES and PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membranes. At different pH values, the number of functional groups on the membrane surface and its ionization degree influenced the surface potential. As shown in Fig. 4(g), the zeta potential values of these four composite membranes decreased with increasing pH in the range of 3–10. Simultaneously, all four composite membranes exhibited negative charge in alkaline and neutral solutions, which attributed to the ionization of –COOH produced by the hydrolysis of residual acyl chloride [45]. Near the isoelectric point where the pH of the solution was acidic, the ionization of –COOH was suppressed, leading to a decrease in the negative charge of the membrane. It was noteworthy that at pH = 7, the absolute values of the surface potentials differed among the four composite membranes, with the potential values being −17.42 mV (PA/PES), −18.81 mV (PA/GO/PES), −20.15 mV (PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES) and −21.32 mV (PA/GOQDs/PES). This difference was related to the presence of more carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in the interlayer, resulting in a lower surface zeta potential of the composite membranes. This result was consistent with the higher oxygen content of composite membranes contained interlayers (Table S1).

To assess the hydrophilicity of the membrane surfaces, water contact angles at the water-air interface were measured. Fig. 4(h) presented the water contact angles of the PA/PES, PA/GO/PES, PA/GOQDs/PES and PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membranes. All four composite membranes exhibited hydrophilic characteristics. The surface hydrophilicity of the membranes was primarily influenced by surface roughness and the content of hydrophilic groups. Although the PA/GO/PES membrane had a higher oxygen content than the PA/PES membrane, its lower roughness resulted in its hydrophilicity (46.6°) slightly worse than that of the PA/PES membrane (43.6°). In contrast, the water contact angles decreased for the PA/GOQDs/PES membrane (42.2°) and the PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES membrane (39.3°) compared to the PA/GO/PES membrane. This reduction could be attributed to the more hydrophilic groups (such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups) on their surfaces under the similar surface roughness conditions.

The salt rejection performance of PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membranes prepared under different PIP concentrations and IP times was shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b). It was evident that the GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane without a PA layer exhibited a low Na2SO4 rejection (34.7 %), whereas PA layer significantly improved the Na2SO4 rejection performance of the PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane. Under the same IP time, the permeation flux of the PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membranes had shown a decreasing trend as the PIP concentration increased, while the corresponding Na2SO4 rejection had initially increased and then stabilized. Under the same PIP concentration conditions, the permeation flux of the PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane decreased with prolonged IP time, while the corresponding Na2SO4 rejection showed two trends: increasing at low PIP concentrations (0.02/0.04 w/v%) and stabilizing after initially increasing at high PIP concentrations (0.06/0.08 w/v%). These results indicated that when using low PIP concentrations and short IP time, the formed PA layer had low thickness and cross-linking degree [46], which was inadequate to effectively condense the pores of the GOQDs-AGQDs interlayer, resulting in a lower Na2SO4 rejection. Conversely, when high PIP concentrations and long IP time were used, the formed PA layer had a thick and complex three-dimensional network structure [47], hindering the transport of water molecules and resulting in a significant decrease in permeation flux. Overall, it was evident that at a PIP concentration of 0.06 w/v% and an IP time of 40s, the prepared PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane exhibited optimal separation performance with a Na2SO4 rejection of 97.1.
% and a permeation flux of 27.2 L ·m−2 ·h−1·bar−1.

Under identical IP conditions (PIP: 0.06 w/v%, IP time: 40s), the desalination performance of PA/PES, PA/GO/PES, PA/GOQDs/PES and PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membranes were evaluated using a feed solution of 2 g ·L−1 Na2SO4 under 4 bar, as shown in Fig. 5(c). All four composite membranes exhibited high Na2SO4 rejection of 94.5 %, 94.0 %, 95.3 % and 97.1 %, respectively. Compared to the permeation flux of PA/PES composite membrane (8.8 L ·m−2 ·h−1·bar−1), the PA/GO/PES composite membrane showed a lower permeation flux (5.7 L ·m−2 h−1·bar−1), while the permeation flux of the PA/GOQDs/PES and PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membranes were 1.6 times (14.4 L ·m−2·h-1·bar−1) and 3.0 times (27.2 L m−2 ·h−1·bar−1) that of the PA/PES composite membrane, respectively.

Based on the above separation performance tests, it was determined that the PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane prepared in this study had successfully surpassed the “trade-off” effect between selectivity and permeability, attaining a high Na2SO4 rejection while significantly improving permeability. The presence of the interlayer in the composite membrane preparation process optimized the storage, release and diffusion of PIP on its surface, thereby regulating the structure of the PA layer. When GO was used as the interlayer to prepare the PA/GO/PES composite membrane, although a thin PA layer was obtained, the stacked GO nanosheets formed long and tortuous transport paths with fewer surface defects, which hindered water molecule transport and ultimately led to poor permeability (Fig. 6 , Fig. S9). In contrast, the use of GOQDs and GOQDs-AGQDs as interlayers to prepare the PA/GOQDs/PES and PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membranes provided more parallel and horizontal nanochannels, partially replacing the intricate three-dimensional network structure of the PA layer. Furthermore, unlike GO and GOQDs interlayer only showed a single diffraction peak within the 5–15° range, which demonstrated that the nanochannel structures formed by these nanosheets were of a single type (GO-GO, GOQDs-GOQDs). The XRD pattern of the GOQDs-AGQDs interlayer exhibited multiple small superposition diffraction peaks within 5–15°range (10.1°, d = 8.75 Å; 10.7°, d = 8.26 Å; 11.1°, d = 7.89 Å), which demonstrated distinct stacking patterns between GOQDs, between GOQDs and AGQDs, and between AGQDs, and constructed different horizonal nanochannels (Fig. S10). The vertical channels were formed by the pores between adjacent GOQDs, AGQDs, GOQDs and AGQDs. Collectively, these horizontal and vertical nanochannels constructed a kind of multidimensional nanochannel within the GOQDs-AGQDs interlayer (Fig. 6). This provision of additional water molecule transport pathways significantly improved permeability.

The rejection performance and permeance of the four composite membranes for different salts (Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2, and NaCl) was shown in Fig. 5(d and e). All composite membranes exhibited a salt rejection order of Na2SO4 > MgSO4 > MgCl2 > NaCl, and the PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane also exhibited the highest permeance. It was widely recognized that ion sieving primarily relied on the synergistic effect of the Donnan effect and size sieving. Clearly, the four prepared composite membranes demonstrated superior rejection performance for divalent salts due to the larger hydrated radius of SO4 2− compared to Cl−. Furthermore, surface zeta potential played a significant role in separation performance [48]. According to the zeta potential results, all four composite membranes exhibited a high negative charge. It was evident that within the pH range of 4–10, the negative charge carried by the four composite membranes (as shown in Fig. 4(g)) exerted a greater repulsive force on divalent ions than on monovalent ions [49,50]. As a result, SO4 2− faced greater resistance than Cl− as they penetrate the membrane and were thus rejected.

Table S2 and Fig. 5(f) compared the performance of the PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane prepared in this study with NF membranes prepared using other types of interlayers [38,51–63]. Compared to the reported NF membranes, the PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane in this study exhibited ideal permeability while maintaining high Na2SO4 rejection. Therefore, this work provided a simple and effective method for producing high-performance NF membranes.

To evaluate the stability of the four composite membranes, long-term permeation tests were conducted using a 2 g ·L−1 Na2SO4 solution under 4 bar pressure, with results shown in Fig. 7 . As the test time progressed, all four composite membranes maintained stable Na2SO4 rejection performance without significant changes. The PA/PES, PA/GO/PES and PA/GOQDs/PES composite membranes maintained a rejection of 94 %, while the PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane maintained a relatively high rejection of 97 %. Additionally, the permeation flux of all four composite membranes remained relatively stable. Notably, the PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane maintained a high permeation flux of 24.2 L ·m−2 h−1·bar−1 after 20 h test and 23.6 L ·m−2 ·h−1·bar−1 after 72 h long-term test (Fig. S11 and 12), indicating that the separation layer had not been ruptured or detached by the high-pressure water flow.

In this work, an ultra-thin i-TFC NF membrane was constructed by inserting hydrophilic, ultrathin and negatively charged GOQDs-AGQDs composite material as an interlayer. The uniform small pore structure and surface hydrophilicity of GOQDs-AGQDs slowed down the diffusion of amine monomers, facilitating a uniform and controllable IP process. The slow and controlled IP reaction contributed to the preparation of intact and ultra-thin polyamide nanofilms at low monomer concentrations, while the presence of multidimensional nanochannels offered possibilities for achieving high permeation flux. Results showed that the optimized PA layer thickness of the PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES membrane could be reduced to 9.4 nm, with a Na2SO4 rejection of 97.1 % and a permeation flux of 27.2 L ·m−2 ·h−1·bar−1. This flux was three times that of the PA/PES composite membrane, five times that of the PA/GO/PES composite membrane, and twice that of the PA/GOQDs/PES composite membrane, achieving simultaneous enhancement of permeability and selectivity. Additionally, the PA/GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membrane exhibited lower surface roughness (23.7 nm) and demonstrated good long-term stability. The proposed IP process based on GOQDs-AGQDs interlayer provides a feasible method for controlling the IP process and adjusting membrane performance.

Yumin Sun: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Hailiang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Hao Cui: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yang Li: Software. Chengxu Sun: Data curation. Kaikai Chen: Investigation. Qinglin Huang: Visualization. Changfa Xiao: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the research funding provided by the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by China Association for Science and Technology (YESS20200231). We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University for the test analysis work.
